params.text:
Making reasonable adjustments

The Equality Act 2010 places a duty to make reasonable adjustments so that people with a disability are not put at a disadvantage compared with others. The goal is equal access – removing barriers so everyone can take part on fair terms.

Adjustments do not need to be expensive or complicated. In everyday care they might include:

  • Providing information in easy-read, large print, braille or another language.
  • Using pictures, gestures or communication aids for someone who finds speech difficult.
  • Adapting the environment – a ramp, a hearing loop, better lighting or an accessible toilet.
  • Allowing extra time, or choosing a quieter moment, for someone who feels anxious or overwhelmed.
  • Adjusting mealtimes, routines or activities to suit an individual's needs and preferences.

Making adjustments is not a favour – it is a legal duty and a core part of person-centred care.

params.alt: A wheelchair ramp — a reasonable adjustment

params.question: A person you support has a learning disability and finds long written documents confusing. What is the most appropriate reasonable adjustment?

answers.text: Provide the information in an easy-read format with pictures, and take time to talk it through.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. Easy-read information and a supportive conversation help the person understand and make their own informed choices.

answers.text: Ask a relative to make the decision on their behalf to save time.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: This removes the person's right to make their own decisions. The aim is to support them to understand, not to decide for them.

answers.text: Give them the standard document and assume they will ask if they don't understand.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: This leaves the barrier in place. The duty is to adapt the information so the person can access it fairly.

answers.text: Skip the information altogether because it might be upsetting.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Withholding information denies the person choice and control over their own care.
